Q1: I tried to keep a steady routine, usually studying in the evenings after work and on weekend mornings. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to actually do things rather than just read, so I'd often work through practice problems or build things with my hands while thinking through concepts. With limited resources, I had to be strategic about when and where I could access materials, often at the library or using public wifi. I aimed for about 16 hours a week, spread out so I wouldn't burn out.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shake things up. I tried to stick to my evening sessions, but when big assignments loomed, I'd squeeze in extra time wherever I could find it. The anxiety I carry made me want to stay ahead, but my motivation being what it is, sometimes I'd fall into a rhythm that was more reactive than proactive. Life demands—work, bills, just surviving on limited resources—definitely pulled me away from my ideal schedule.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I knew there was something I needed to complete or check, like assignment deadlines or new materials being posted. Sometimes the gamified tasks they introduced actually drew me in more than I expected—earning points or badges gave me a little dopamine hit that helped on low motivation days. I also logged in just to check discussion posts, since connecting with classmates helped with the sadness I've been carrying around lately.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks, logging in became almost automatic, like checking my phone in the morning. The gamified elements really helped with that—I'd want to see my progress and keep my streak going. It felt less like a chore and more like a small accomplishment each day. Even when I wasn't feeling motivated, the habit was already there, so I'd at least pop in briefly.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me—I watched nearly 200 minutes worth because I could pause, rewind, and really absorb things at my own pace. The interactive practice activities were also huge since I learn by doing, not just reading. Discussion posts helped too because explaining things to peers or reading their perspectives made concepts stick better. Anything hands-on felt worth my time.
Q6: I'll admit I skipped over some of the longer reading PDFs, especially when they felt dense and theoretical. With my learning style, just reading walls of text doesn't work well for me—I need to engage physically with the material somehow. Some supplementary resources felt like nice-to-haves rather than essentials, so when time was tight, those got pushed aside. I also ignored some optional quizzes when my anxiety was spiking and I didn't want another thing to potentially fail at.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my grades or when it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The gamified tasks were surprisingly effective because they made even small activities feel rewarding. If I could do something hands-on or interactive, I was much more likely to engage fully. Basically, if it moved me forward in a tangible way, I was in.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—there was less pressure, which was nice for my anxiety, but also less motivation to actually do them. Required ones had this weight to them that pushed me through even when I didn't feel like it. The gamified optional stuff was the exception though; the points and badges made them feel worthwhile even without the grade pressure. Sometimes the optional activities were where I learned the most because I wasn't stressed about performing.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded PDFs and videos whenever I could because my internet access isn't always reliable at home, and I can't afford data overages. I'd print materials at the library and study from paper, making notes in the margins and working through problems by hand. I also used free YouTube videos and external resources to supplement what the course offered. A lot of my real learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many nights where I sat with printed notes and my notebook, working through concepts by physically writing and rewriting until they made sense. I'd pace around my apartment talking through ideas out loud—that kinesthetic approach really works for me but obviously leaves no digital trace. Study groups with classmates happened in person at the library too. The LMS would show nothing for those hours of genuine engagement.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video lecture once, then watch it multiple times offline over the following days. Same with PDFs—I'd access them once to download, then study from the printed version for a week. My low resources situation meant I had to be efficient with my internet usage, so one download often translated to many hours of offline study. The LMS only captured that initial access, not the repeated engagement.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, usually when my anxiety spiked around midterms or when personal stuff got heavy. The sadness I've been dealing with made some weeks feel like I was moving through fog. When money got especially tight, worrying about basic needs made it hard to focus on coursework. Those were the times my attendance dipped and I fell behind on logging in regularly.
Q13: Competing responsibilities hit hard—working to pay bills while studying full-time is exhausting. When I was fatigued, I'd log into the LMS but barely absorb anything, just going through the motions. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules, which probably showed up as lower engagement in specific areas. The workload sometimes felt overwhelming, and my moderate resilience meant I'd bounce back eventually, but not always quickly.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about rent or just feeling down. I'd scroll through materials without really processing them, just trying to feel like I was doing something productive. Those sessions probably looked like engagement in the data but were pretty hollow. It's hard to be present when anxiety is running the show in your head.
Q15: I postponed some assignments even knowing they were important, especially when my motivation was at rock bottom. The anxiety created this weird paralysis where I knew I needed to do something but couldn't start. Sometimes I'd avoid discussion posts because putting my thoughts out there felt vulnerable when I was already feeling sad. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become three days later.
Q16: Deadlines were honestly my main driver when motivation failed me. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to power through, even if it meant late nights. The LMS deadline reminders helped keep me on track when my own internal motivation wasn't cutting it. My engagement definitely spiked around due dates—you'd probably see clear patterns in my login times correlating with assignment deadlines.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback boosted my mood and made me want to engage more, which helped given my generally sad state. But critical feedback sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me hesitant to submit the next assignment. The gamified feedback—points and badges—was easier to handle emotionally than written critiques. I learned to check feedback when I was in a better headspace.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which helped since my own motivation wasn't always reliable. Having clear modules and progression made it easier to know what to tackle next. The gamified elements added structure that appealed to my need for tangible progress markers. Weekly rhythms helped me build habits even when I didn't feel like studying.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier—they turned studying into something almost game-like, which helped on low motivation days. The video content being downloadable was crucial for someone with my resource constraints. What made it harder was when materials were only available online without download options, or when discussion requirements felt forced. Dense text-heavy sections were tough for my kinesthetic learning style.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to produce work that meets their standards—that anxiety about not being good enough can be paralyzing. They might be engaging deeply with materials but hitting walls when it comes to actually submitting. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make consumption easier than production. High activity doesn't always mean someone has the mental bandwidth to complete assignments.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. My exam score was really high even though my attendance wasn't perfect, because a lot of my effective studying happened offline. Some people are efficient learners who don't need to be constantly clicking around the LMS to absorb material. Or they might have strong prior knowledge that lets them succeed without as much visible engagement. The LMS doesn't capture studying from printed notes or thinking through problems while doing dishes.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, completion rates for specific tasks. They're good at capturing digital breadcrumbs and identifying students who might be struggling with access. But they miss the quality of engagement, the offline studying, the emotional state behind the clicks. They can't tell if someone watched a video attentively or had it playing in the background while crying about their life.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just checking boxes. It's when I'm genuinely curious and working through problems because I want to get it, not just to pass. For me, it involves physical engagement—writing, doing, practicing—not just passive consumption. It also means showing up mentally, being present even when anxiety or sadness are trying to pull me away.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my offline study hours, which were substantial given my resource limitations. It wouldn't capture the emotional labor of pushing through anxiety to submit assignments or the sadness that colored my entire semester. My peer study sessions in person, my handwritten notes, my pacing around working through concepts verbally—none of that appears in any log. The data shows what I clicked, not who I am or how hard I fought to succeed despite everything working against me.
